events.text: The patient is a 38-year-old woman named Marlena Okonkwo. She has a family history significant for a maternal aunt diagnosed with ovarian cancer at age 52 and a maternal grandmother with breast cancer at age 60. She is of West African descent, nulliparous, premenopausal, and works as a high school biology teacher. She has no significant past medical history aside from mild intermittent asthma managed with an albuterol inhaler. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally.

At age 38 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0, AJCC 8th edition) invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC), grade 3, of the left breast, upper outer quadrant. ICD-O-3 site code C50.4 (Upper-outer quadrant of breast), histology code 8500/3 (Infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS). Tumor size by imaging: 3.1 cm. Lymph node status: 1 clinically palpable ipsilateral axillary lymph node, biopsy-confirmed metastatic carcinoma. Receptor status: Estrogen receptor (ER) negative (Allred score 0/8), Progesterone receptor (PR) negative (Allred score 0/8), HER2 negative (IHC 1+, FISH not performed). Ki-67 proliferation index: 85%. Nuclear grade: 3. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Summary stage: Regional. Confirmation method: Positive histology via core needle biopsy. Laterality: Left. Tumor location: Upper outer quadrant. No evidence of distant metastasis on staging workup.

At age 38, the patient had a core needle biopsy pathology report from the left breast upper outer quadrant mass and left axillary lymph node fine-needle aspiration (FNA). The breast core biopsy showed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9: tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Tumor cells were arranged in solid nests with central necrosis. Stromal tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) were noted at 40%. Lymphovascular invasion was identified. ER: negative (0% staining, intensity 0). PR: negative (0% staining, intensity 0). HER2: 1+ by IHC (negative). Ki-67: 85%. The axillary lymph node FNA showed metastatic carcinoma consistent with primary breast origin. PD-L1 expression by combined positive score (CPS): 15 (22C3 assay, positive threshold >= 10).

At age 38, the patient had a diagnostic bilateral mammogram and ultrasound of the left breast and left axilla for staging purposes. Mammogram showed a 3.1 cm irregular, high-density mass in the left upper outer quadrant with spiculated margins and associated microcalcifications. No additional suspicious lesions in the right breast. Ultrasound confirmed a 3.1 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic, irregular mass at the 10 o'clock position, 3 cm from the nipple, with posterior acoustic enhancement. One enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring 2.0 cm with loss of fatty hilum was identified. BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). No skin thickening or nipple involvement. Cancer status: present. No distant metastatic sites identified.

At age 38, the patient had a staging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, as well as a bone scan for distant metastasis evaluation. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, pleural effusion, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Left axillary lymphadenopathy confirmed (largest node 2.0 cm). CT abdomen/pelvis: No liver lesions, adrenal masses, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No ascites. Bone scan: No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastases. Overall impression: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Staging consistent with locoregional disease, Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0). Cancer status: present, locoregional only.

At age 38 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core needle biopsy (primary tumor). Tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) panel (324-gene panel) showed the following findings: Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic, variant allele frequency 48%), PIK3CA wild-type, BRCA1 p.E1250* (pathogenic germline-confirmed somatic loss, VAF 51%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion), RB1 p.Q670* (pathogenic, VAF 39%). Co-mutations and other findings: MYC amplification (copy number 14), CCND1 wild-type, CDK4 wild-type, CDKN2A p.P114L (VAF 22%), RAD51C wild-type, PALB2 wild-type, CHEK2 wild-type, ATM wild-type, FANCA wild-type, BRIP1 wild-type, NBN wild-type. Fusions: none detected. Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 9.2 mutations/megabase (intermediate). PD-L1 CPS: 15 (confirmed on IHC). Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 54 (positive, threshold >= 42). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 22%. No NTRK, ALK, or RET fusions detected. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed on reflexed germline testing (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*, reported separately to genetic counseling).

At age 38, the patient had an oncology new patient consultation. She presented with a self-detected left breast mass noticed 6 weeks prior to presentation. Examination showed a firm, irregular, 3 cm palpable mass in the left upper outer quadrant, non-tender, with fixation to surrounding tissue. A single palpable left axillary lymph node approximately 2 cm was noted. No skin changes, nipple discharge, or contralateral findings. Performance status ECOG 0. Following tumor board discussion, the decision was made to pursue neoadjuvant systemic therapy given T2N1 disease and TNBC histology. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed; genetic counseling referral placed. Fertility preservation counseling offered and patient deferred (nulliparous, declined oocyte cryopreservation due to time constraints). Plan: neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks plus dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 every 2 weeks (ddAC) x 4 cycles, followed by paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 weekly x 12 weeks with concurrent pembrolizumab every 3 weeks, then definitive surgery. Cancer present. No response assessment yet (pre-treatment baseline).

At age 38 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 of each 21-day cycle, concurrently with dose-dense doxorubicin (Adriamycin) 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV every 14 days (ddAC), for 4 cycles. Pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC was administered on day 2 of each ddAC cycle for primary prophylaxis of febrile neutropenia. Pembrolizumab cycle 1 day 1 administered concurrently with ddAC cycle 1. Total duration of ddAC phase: approximately 8 weeks. This was administered as part of the KEYNOTE-522-inspired neoadjuvant regimen for stage IIB TNBC with PD-L1 CPS >= 10.

At age 38 (approximately 5 weeks into neoadjuvant therapy, during ddAC cycle 3), the patient developed an immune-related adverse event (irAE): grade 2 immune-mediated hepatitis. Liver function tests showed ALT 186 U/L (4.2x ULN), AST 152 U/L (3.8x ULN), total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL (within normal limits), alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated at 98 U/L. The patient was asymptomatic. Pembrolizumab was held. Work-up included hepatitis B and C serologies (negative), ANA (1:80 speckled pattern), anti-smooth muscle antibody (negative), ceruloplasmin (normal). Liver ultrasound showed no ductal dilation or masses. The patient was started on prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg/day). ddAC cycle 3 was administered on schedule as the hepatitis was attributed to pembrolizumab. LFTs normalized within 3 weeks. Prednisone tapered over 4 weeks. Pembrolizumab was resumed after confirmed LFT normalization.

At age 38 (during ddAC phase, cycle 2), the patient developed grade 2 chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) despite scheduled ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aprepitant premedication. She required an additional rescue lorazepam dose and was seen in the infusion clinic for IV hydration on one occasion. No hospitalization required. Anti-emetic regimen was optimized for subsequent cycles with the addition of olanzapine 10 mg on days 1-4. Additionally, the patient experienced grade 1 alopecia beginning after cycle 1, which progressed to grade 2 complete alopecia by cycle 2 as expected with doxorubicin-based therapy.

At age 38, the patient had a mid-treatment response assessment MRI of the left breast and axilla after completion of ddAC x 4 cycles (prior to initiation of weekly paclitaxel). Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI showed the primary left breast mass had decreased from 3.1 cm to 1.8 cm (42% reduction in longest dimension). The previously identified left axillary lymph node had decreased in size from 2.0 cm to 1.0 cm with partial restoration of fatty hilum. No new suspicious lesions identified in either breast. No skin thickening. Impression: Partial response to neoadjuvant therapy. Residual tumor with radiographic partial response (per RECIST 1.1 criteria, partial response). Cancer status: present, responding. Recommendation: proceed with planned weekly paclitaxel plus pembrolizumab phase.

At age 38 years, the patient received the second phase of neoadjuvant therapy: paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks (administered days 1, 8, 15 of each 21-day cycle, for 4 cycles), with concurrent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 of each 21-day cycle. Total of 12 paclitaxel infusions administered. This phase followed completion of ddAC x 4 cycles. Pembrolizumab was resumed after confirmed LFT normalization following prior irAE. Premedication with diphenhydramine, famotidine, and dexamethasone administered prior to each paclitaxel infusion.

At age 38 (during weekly paclitaxel phase, approximately week 8 of 12), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the bilateral hands and feet with numbness and tingling, impairing fine motor tasks (difficulty buttoning clothes, using a keyboard). Paclitaxel dose was reduced by 20% (to 64 mg/m2) for the remaining 4 weekly doses. The patient was counseled on neuropathy precautions. Duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated for symptomatic management, titrated to 60 mg daily. No dose discontinuation required. Grade improved to grade 1 by the time of surgical planning.

At age 38, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at completion of the full neoadjuvant regimen (ddAC x 4 cycles + weekly paclitaxel x 12 weeks + concurrent pembrolizumab x 8 cycles). Physical examination showed a residual palpable left breast mass estimated at approximately 1.5 cm, slightly firm. Left axillary lymph node was no longer palpable. Performance status ECOG 0-1 (mild fatigue, grade 1 neuropathy). The patient was referred for surgical planning. Plan: proceed to definitive surgery -- left lumpectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy vs. axillary lymph node dissection, based on intraoperative findings. Pembrolizumab was held in the perioperative period per institutional protocol. Cancer present with partial response to neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 38 years, the patient underwent a left partial mastectomy (lumpectomy) with wire localization of the residual tumor bed plus sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) under general anesthesia. Intraoperative findings: residual firm mass in the left upper outer quadrant, approximately 1.5 cm by palpation. Three sentinel lymph nodes were identified and excised (blue dye and radiocolloid technique). Intraoperative frozen section of sentinel lymph node 1 showed metastatic carcinoma (micrometastasis, 0.4 mm focus). Given micrometastatic involvement, axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) was performed (levels I and II). ALND yielded 14 additional lymph nodes. Surgical margins were obtained in all six directions. Operative time: 3 hours 10 minutes. Estimated blood loss: 80 mL. No intraoperative complications. Patient recovered uneventfully and was discharged on postoperative day 1.

At age 38, the patient had a definitive surgical pathology report from left lumpectomy and axillary lymph node dissection. Gross specimen: Lumpectomy specimen 4.5 x 3.8 x 2.2 cm, with clip at tumor bed. Microscopic findings: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.4 cm in greatest dimension. Extensive treatment effect noted with large areas of fibrosis, ghost cell formations, and foamy macrophage infiltration (approximately 55% treatment-related fibrosis). Residual viable tumor cells comprising approximately 45% of the original tumor bed area. Lymphovascular invasion not identified in residual tumor. Margins: all margins negative (closest margin: posterior, 3 mm). Lymph nodes: 1 of 17 nodes positive (1 sentinel node with micrometastasis 0.4 mm, isolated tumor cell cluster; 0 of 14 ALND nodes positive, 0 of 2 remaining sentinel nodes positive). ypT1c ypN0(i+) -- residual pathologic stage ypT1c ypN0(i+) M0. Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) class: RCB-II (intermediate residual disease). Miller-Payne grade: 3 (30-90% reduction in tumor cellularity). ER, PR, HER2 on residual tumor: negative (triple-negative confirmed). Ki-67 on residual tumor: 60% (down from 85% at diagnosis). Stromal TILs in residual tumor: 10% (decreased from 40% at diagnosis).

At age 38 years, the patient received adjuvant whole breast radiation therapy (WBRT) to the left breast following lumpectomy, with a sequential boost to the tumor bed. Total dose: 50 Gy in 25 fractions to the whole left breast (2 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5 weeks), followed by a sequential electron boost of 10 Gy in 5 fractions to the lumpectomy cavity (total tumor bed dose 60 Gy). Concurrent regional nodal irradiation (RNI) was administered to the left supraclavicular, infraclavicular, and axillary (levels I-III) nodal regions given node-positive disease, to a dose of 50 Gy in 25 fractions. Treatment planning: 3D conformal with tangential fields and direct supraclavicular field. Normal tissue constraints met for lung (V20 < 15%) and heart (mean dose < 2 Gy, left-sided). No acute grade 3+ toxicity. Grade 2 radiation dermatitis developed in weeks 4-5, managed with topical silver sulfadiazine and moisture barrier cream.

At age 38 (during adjuvant radiation therapy, weeks 4-5 of WBRT), the patient developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left breast with erythema, moist desquamation in the inframammary fold, and moderate pain (pain score 5/10). Managed with topical silver sulfadiazine cream twice daily, moisture barrier ointment, and oral ibuprofen 400 mg TID as needed. No treatment interruption required. Skin toxicity resolved to grade 1 by 3 weeks post-radiation completion. Additionally, mild fatigue (grade 1-2) persisted throughout radiation, attributed to cumulative treatment fatigue. The patient continued working part-time during radiation.

At age 38 years, the patient received adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily on days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle for 6 cycles (total approximately 18 weeks of capecitabine therapy), in accordance with the CREATE-X trial data for TNBC patients with residual disease after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Additionally, adjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks was resumed and continued for a planned total of 1 year (17 cycles total including neoadjuvant doses; approximately 9 additional cycles in the adjuvant setting). Capecitabine and pembrolizumab were initiated approximately 3-4 weeks after completion of radiation therapy. The patient was counseled on hand-foot syndrome prevention with urea cream and dose modification criteria.

At age 38 (approximately 6 weeks into adjuvant capecitabine, capecitabine cycle 2), the patient developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome, HFS) with painful erythema, swelling, and tenderness of the bilateral palms and soles, interfering with daily activities (difficulty standing for prolonged periods in the classroom). Capecitabine was dose-reduced by 25% (to 750 mg/m2 BID) per standard dose modification guidelines. Topical urea 20% cream was applied twice daily to affected areas; pyridoxine 200 mg daily was added. HFS improved to grade 1 within 2 weeks. Capecitabine was continued at the reduced dose for cycles 3-6 without further dose modifications. No additional capecitabine dose reductions required.

At age 38-39, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at completion of adjuvant capecitabine (6 cycles complete). She tolerated the regimen reasonably well with capecitabine dose reduction for HFS as noted. Pembrolizumab maintenance continued (currently on cycle 14 total). Physical examination: well-appearing, ECOG 0. Left breast: no palpable mass, expected post-lumpectomy changes. No axillary adenopathy bilaterally. Pertinent labs: CBC within normal limits, LFTs normal, TSH 6.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, upper limit normal 4.5). Free T4 0.82 ng/dL (low). Assessment: likely pembrolizumab-associated primary hypothyroidism (grade 1-2 irAE). Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated; pembrolizumab continued without interruption. TSH normalized at subsequent visit. Patient counseled regarding ongoing germline BRCA1 mutation implications for risk-reducing surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy consideration; deferred to age 40). Cancer status: no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 39, the patient had a surveillance bilateral breast MRI (right breast full, left breast post-lumpectomy) approximately 12 months after surgery. Right breast: No suspicious enhancement, no masses, no lymphadenopathy. Left breast: Post-surgical and post-radiation changes in the left upper outer quadrant with expected architectural distortion, fat necrosis, and clip artifact. No new suspicious enhancement. No enlarged axillary, internal mammary, or supraclavicular lymph nodes. Background parenchymal enhancement: moderate. Impression: No evidence of local or regional recurrence. No new suspicious findings. Cancer status: no evidence of disease.

At age 39, the patient completed her planned 1 year (17 total cycles) of pembrolizumab. Final pembrolizumab dose administered. She reported ongoing grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (feet only, stable, not worsening). Hypothyroidism well-controlled on levothyroxine 50 mcg. She returned to full-time teaching. She underwent genetic counseling follow-up; her two sisters were referred for BRCA1 testing. Patient declined risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) at this time citing desire to discuss with partner. She will continue annual breast MRI surveillance and semi-annual oncology visits. Plan: surveillance with clinical exams every 6 months, annual bilateral breast MRI, annual mammogram (contralateral breast), CA 27-29 not routinely monitored per guidelines. No evidence of disease at this visit.

At age 39 (approximately 18 months post-surgery), the patient had annual surveillance imaging: diagnostic mammogram of the right breast and left breast (post-lumpectomy views) plus bilateral breast MRI. Mammogram: Right breast negative. Left breast: stable post-surgical changes, clip artifact at lumpectomy site, no new calcifications. MRI: Right breast no suspicious lesions. Left breast: expected post-treatment changes, no new enhancement. Left axilla: no lymphadenopathy. Liver: not evaluated on breast MRI. Cancer status: no evidence of disease. Note: patient also reported new right-sided rib pain rated 3/10, described as constant and non-pleuritic. Physical exam was unremarkable. Given BRCA1 germline mutation and residual disease at surgery (RCB-II), further evaluation ordered.

At age 39 (18 months post-surgery, prompted by new rib pain), the patient had a whole-body bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for evaluation of possible distant recurrence. Bone scan: Multiple foci of increased radiotracer uptake identified: right 5th rib (posterior), T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing. Findings suspicious for osseous metastases. CT chest: No pulmonary parenchymal nodules or masses. Right 5th rib lesion: lytic-appearing, 1.5 cm, with cortical disruption. CT abdomen: Liver shows two hypoattenuating lesions, right lobe, measuring 1.2 cm and 0.8 cm. No adenopathy above diaphragm. CT pelvis: Right iliac wing lesion 1.8 cm, lytic, with subtle cortical disruption. Impression: Highly suspicious for distant metastatic disease involving the osseous skeleton and liver. Cancer status: present, likely metastatic. Recommend biopsy confirmation.

At age 39, the patient underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right iliac wing lesion for pathologic confirmation of suspected distant recurrence. Pathology: Metastatic carcinoma, consistent with breast primary. Histology: poorly differentiated carcinoma with solid architecture, high nuclear grade. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative (0%), PR negative (0%), HER2 negative (IHC 1+, not amplified), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, GATA3 positive, mammaglobin focally positive, TTF-1 negative. Ki-67: 72%. Diagnosis: Metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma to the right iliac wing. PD-L1 CPS on metastatic specimen: 8 (below threshold of 10 for first-line pembrolizumab in metastatic setting). Liquid biopsy (circulating tumor DNA, ctDNA) from concurrent blood draw showed BRCA1 p.E1250* (VAF 4.2%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 3.8%), PTEN loss (detected), new PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 1.1%, acquired mutation not present on primary tumor NGS), MYC amplification detected.

At age 39 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on the metastatic iliac wing biopsy specimen (bone metastasis, right iliac wing). Tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) showed: TP53 p.R248W (pathogenic, VAF 45%), BRCA1 p.E1250* (pathogenic, VAF 49%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, confirmed), PIK3CA p.H1047R (pathogenic, acquired, VAF 18% -- not present on primary tumor), RB1 p.Q670* (pathogenic, VAF 38%), MYC amplification (copy number 18, increased from 14 on primary), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (new finding, not detected at primary), CDK4 amplification (copy number 8, new finding -- co-occurring with CDKN2A loss, consistent with CDK4/6 pathway activation). ESR1 wild-type. FGFR2 wild-type. No NTRK fusions. No ALK fusions. Microsatellite status: MSS. TMB: 11.4 mutations/megabase (intermediate-high). HRD score: 61 (positive, increased from 54 at primary). PD-L1 CPS: 8 on metastatic specimen (decreased from 15 on primary). The acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R mutation and CDK4 amplification represent new targetable alterations in the metastatic setting, suggesting clonal evolution during treatment.

At age 39, the patient had an oncology visit for counseling and treatment planning following confirmed distant metastatic TNBC recurrence (bone and liver). ECOG performance status 1 (rib pain managed with scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen, now additionally prescribed opioid for breakthrough pain control; radiation oncology consulted for palliative radiation to symptomatic rib lesion). Disease-free interval from surgery to metastatic recurrence: approximately 18 months. The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Key considerations: (1) prior pembrolizumab exposure (completed 17 cycles adjuvant, now PD-L1 CPS 8 on metastatic specimen, immune exhaustion possible); (2) germline BRCA1 mutation -- PARP inhibitor (olaparib or talazoparib) indicated; (3) acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R -- potential alpelisib consideration in future; (4) HRD positive. Treatment plan for first-line metastatic: olaparib 300 mg BID as monotherapy given germline BRCA1 mutation and prior platinum-free interval, per OlympiAD trial data. Palliative radiation for right 5th rib. Referral to palliative care. Cancer present, metastatic.

At age 39 years, the patient received palliative radiation therapy to the right 5th posterior rib for pain control. Dose: 20 Gy in 5 fractions (4 Gy/fraction, delivered over 5 days) using 3D conformal planning with opposed lateral fields. Treatment intent: palliative (pain control, fracture prevention). The patient tolerated palliative radiation well with no acute grade 3+ toxicities. Pain at the rib site improved from 5/10 to 1/10 within 2 weeks of treatment completion. No radiation skin reactions. Concurrent olaparib was held for 1 week during radiation per institutional protocol and resumed on day 8.

At age 39 years, the patient initiated first-line treatment for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer with germline BRCA1 mutation: olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg orally twice daily (continuous dosing). This was selected based on the OlympiAD trial evidence for germline BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer. Concurrent bone-modifying agent: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 4 weeks initiated for bone metastases (along with calcium 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800 IU/day supplementation, with baseline dental evaluation completed). Olaparib started after completion of palliative rib radiation and after a 48-hour washout.

At age 39 (approximately 3 months after initiation of olaparib for metastatic disease), the patient had restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus bone scan for response assessment. CT abdomen: Right lobe hepatic lesions stable to slightly decreased -- the 1.2 cm lesion now measures 0.9 cm and the 0.8 cm lesion is now 0.6 cm. No new liver lesions. CT chest: No new pulmonary lesions. Right 5th rib: post-radiation changes, no new fracture. Bone scan: Previously identified foci of uptake at T10 and right iliac wing are stable. No new sites of radiotracer uptake. No new bone lesions on CT. Impression: Partial response to olaparib therapy in the liver; stable osseous disease. Overall assessment: partial response per RECIST 1.1 (combined liver lesion reduction > 30%). Cancer status: present, responding. Plan: continue olaparib at current dose, continue zoledronic acid, reassess in 3 months.

At age 39-40, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit at 6 months of olaparib therapy for metastatic TNBC. She reported grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 nausea (managed with ondansetron PRN), tolerated well overall. Complete blood count showed mild anemia: hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (grade 1, baseline 12.8 g/dL), MCV 98 fL (macrocytic pattern consistent with PARP inhibitor effect). Reticulocyte count low-normal. No dose reduction required (threshold for reduction is hemoglobin < 8 g/dL or symptomatic). Iron studies: ferritin 68 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 24% (no iron deficiency). The patient reported that her sister tested positive for BRCA1 and was referred for risk-reduction counseling. Performance status ECOG 1. No new symptoms. She decided to proceed with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for ovarian cancer risk reduction given BRCA1 mutation (scheduled with gynecologic oncology). Cancer present (known metastatic disease), responding to olaparib.

scenario_blurb: Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) stage IIB in a 45-year-old woman, diagnosed after palpable mass and core needle biopsy showing grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR/HER2 negative, Ki-67 85%. Treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense AC followed by paclitaxel, then surgery. Mixed pathologic response with residual disease, followed by adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance.
